title: Core Principles
overview: Microservices architecture structures an application as a collection of small, independently deployable services.
Each service owns a single business capability, its own data, and its own release cycle.
Services talk to each other over the network instead of through in-process method calls.
why: A single growing codebase eventually becomes hard for many teams to change safely at once.
Different parts of a system often need to scale, deploy, or evolve at different rates.
Splitting along business boundaries lets teams own, deploy, and scale their piece independently.
problem: A **monolith** starts simple but grows into one large deployable unit over time.
Every team shares the same codebase, the same build, and the same release train.
One team's bug or slow deploy can block or break every other team's release.
Scaling means scaling the entire application, even if only one module is under load.
Adopting a new tech stack for one module means rewriting the whole monolith.

intro: Microservices are built around a small set of non-negotiable principles.

topics.heading: Fundamentals
points: **Single Responsibility** — each microservice owns one business capability, enabling independent development, testing, and scaling.
**Loose Coupling** — services interact only through well-defined APIs; internal implementation changes must never break consumers.
**Independent Deployment** — each service has its own CI/CD pipeline, release cycle, and versioning, with no coordinated deployments needed.
**Decentralized Data** — each service owns its database (**Database per Service** pattern); no direct DB access across service boundaries.
**Domain-Driven Design** — use **Bounded Contexts** to define service boundaries, modeled with aggregate roots, entities, and value objects.

topics.heading: Monolith vs Microservices
points: **Monolith** — a single deployable unit; simple to develop initially, but scaling and team autonomy become bottlenecks as it grows.
**Microservices** — a distributed system of small services enabling polyglot tech stacks, independent scaling, and team ownership.
**Migration Strategy** — the **Strangler Fig** pattern incrementally extracts services from a monolith, starting with least-coupled modules first.
**When to Use Monolith** — small teams, early-stage products, simple domains; avoid premature microservices (the distributed monolith anti-pattern).
**When to Use Microservices** — large teams, complex domains, and a need for independent scaling and deployment, accepting operational complexity in return.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Monolith: Order, Payment, Inventory in one codebase"] --> B{"Identify least-coupled module"}
    B --> C["Extract Payment as its own service"]
    C --> D["Route Payment traffic through Strangler Facade"]
    D --> E["Extract Inventory service"]
    E --> F["Extract Order service"]
    F --> G["Monolith fully replaced by microservices"]
architectureFlow.caption: Strangler Fig migration: services are extracted one at a time behind a facade until nothing of the monolith remains.

simpleExample.scenario: Splitting a single `OrderApp` monolith into two independently deployable services.
simpleExample.input: A customer submits an order with payment details.
simpleExample.execution: `OrderService` no longer calls payment logic in-process — it calls `PaymentClient`, which makes a network call to a separately deployed `payment-service`.
simpleExample.output: The order is saved only after the independently deployed `payment-service` confirms the charge succeeded.
simpleExample.explanation: The two capabilities can now be deployed, scaled, and owned by separate teams, at the cost of a network call replacing a method call.

realWorldExample.scenario: An e-commerce platform organized as bounded contexts: `order-service`, `payment-service`, `inventory-service`, and `merchant-service`.
realWorldExample.input: A checkout request arrives at `order-service` for a `Cart` belonging to a `Customer`.
realWorldExample.execution: `order-service` persists to its own **order database**, then calls `payment-service`'s API to charge the `Merchant`'s configured `Gateway`. Each service is deployed, scaled, and owned independently.
realWorldExample.output: An `Order` record with status `CONFIRMED`, created without `order-service` ever touching the payment database directly.
realWorldExample.explanation: This mirrors **Database per Service** and **Bounded Context**: `order-service` and `payment-service` can be released on different days by different teams without coordinating a single deployment.

bestPractices: Use the **Strangler Fig** pattern to migrate incrementally rather than a risky big-bang rewrite.
Define service boundaries around **Bounded Contexts**, not around technical layers like "database" or "UI".
Start with a monolith for a new, simple, small-team product — extract services only once real pain appears.
commonMistakes: Splitting services by technical layer (e.g. a separate "database service") instead of by business capability.
Building a **distributed monolith** — many services that must still be deployed together because they're tightly coupled.
Adopting microservices prematurely for a small team with a simple domain, taking on operational cost with no real benefit.
interviewTips: If asked "why microservices", lead with team autonomy and independent scaling — not "it's more modern".
Be ready to name the trade-off explicitly: microservices trade simplicity for scalability and team independence.
Know the **Strangler Fig** pattern by name — it's the standard answer for "how do you migrate a monolith".

faq.q: Should every new project start with microservices?
faq.a: No. Most teams should start with a well-structured monolith and extract services only once specific modules show real scaling or team-ownership pain.

It's microservices in name only — services are still so tightly coupled that they must be deployed together, giving you all the operational complexity of a distributed system with none of the independence benefits.

faq.q: How do Bounded Contexts relate to service boundaries?
faq.a: A Bounded Context from Domain-Driven Design defines where one model's meaning ends and another begins — it's the recommended way to decide where one microservice ends and the next begins.

summary: Microservices split an application into small, independently deployable services, each owning one business capability and its own data.
The core principles are single responsibility, loose coupling, independent deployment, and decentralized data ownership.
Monoliths are simpler to start with; microservices pay off once team size, domain complexity, and scaling needs outgrow a single deployable unit.
quickRevision: Microservices = small, independently deployable services, each owning one business capability.
Database per Service — no service reaches directly into another service's database.
Bounded Context (DDD) is the recommended way to draw service boundaries.
Strangler Fig pattern — migrate a monolith incrementally, least-coupled modules first.
Distributed monolith = anti-pattern where services still must deploy together.
Use monolith for small teams/simple domains; use microservices for large teams/complex domains.
Independent deployment means each service has its own CI/CD pipeline and versioning.

beginner: What is a microservice, in one sentence?
What is the main difference between a monolith and microservices?
Why does each microservice need its own database?
intermediate: What is the Strangler Fig pattern, and when would you use it?
How do Bounded Contexts help define microservice boundaries?
What operational costs do microservices introduce that a monolith doesn't have?
advanced: How would you decide whether a given module should be extracted into its own service?
What is a distributed monolith, and what causes a system to end up as one?
How would you migrate a tightly-coupled monolith to microservices without a big-bang rewrite?

title: Communication Patterns
overview: Once an application is split into services, those services must talk to each other over the network.
Communication can be synchronous (request waits for a response) or asynchronous (fire-and-forget via events).
An API Gateway typically sits in front of all of this as the single entry point for external clients.
why: In a monolith, one module calling another is just a method call — fast and always available.
In microservices, that same call crosses a network, which can be slow, fail, or time out.
Different use cases need different trade-offs between immediacy and resilience, hence multiple communication styles.
problem: A naive approach makes every inter-service call synchronous, like a local method call.
This creates **tight temporal coupling** — the caller blocks until the callee responds.
If the callee is slow or down, the failure cascades up the entire call chain.
Clients calling many services directly also have to know every service's location and API shape.

intro: There are three broad areas: synchronous calls, asynchronous messaging, and the gateway that fronts them.

topics.heading: Synchronous Communication
points: **REST (HTTP/JSON)** — the most common inter-service communication; simple, stateless, cacheable, using proper HTTP methods and status codes.
**gRPC** — high-performance RPC using Protocol Buffers, with binary serialization, HTTP/2, and bidirectional streaming; ideal for internal service-to-service calls.
**OpenFeign** — declarative REST client for Spring: `@FeignClient(name = "user-service")` with interface methods, auto load-balanced via Spring Cloud.
**WebClient** — non-blocking, reactive HTTP client from Spring WebFlux with streaming support; preferred over `RestTemplate` (deprecated in Spring 6).
**Drawbacks** — tight temporal coupling, cascading failures, and increased latency; use async patterns when a real-time response isn't required.

topics.heading: Asynchronous Communication
points: **Message Queues** — decouple producer from consumer using RabbitMQ (AMQP) or ActiveMQ, point-to-point or pub/sub, guaranteeing delivery with acknowledgments.
**Apache Kafka** — a distributed event streaming platform with topics, partitions, and consumer groups; high throughput, durable, replayable event log.
**Event-Driven Architecture** — services emit domain events that other services react to asynchronously, enabling loose coupling and eventual consistency.
**CQRS** — Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates read and write models, often paired with Event Sourcing for audit trails.
**Saga Pattern** — manages distributed transactions across services via **Choreography** (events) or **Orchestration** (central coordinator), using compensating transactions on failure.

topics.heading: API Gateway
points: **Purpose** — a single entry point for clients that handles routing, authentication, rate limiting, SSL termination, and request aggregation.
**Spring Cloud Gateway** — a reactive gateway built on WebFlux with route predicates, pre/post filters, circuit breaker integration, and request rate limiting.
**Backend for Frontend (BFF)** — separate gateways per client type (web, mobile, IoT), each tailoring API responses to its client's needs.
**Service Mesh** — an infrastructure layer for service-to-service communication (Istio, Linkerd) that handles mTLS, retries, and observability without app code changes.
**API Versioning** — via URL path (`/v1/users`), header (`Accept-Version`), or query param; the gateway can route to different service versions.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Synchronous
    A["Order Service"] -->|"REST call, waits"| B["Payment Service"]
    B -->|"response"| A
    end
    subgraph Asynchronous
    C["Order Service"] -->|"publishes OrderPlaced event"| D["Kafka Topic"]
    D -->|"consumes event"| E["Inventory Service"]
    D -->|"consumes event"| F["Notification Service"]
    end
architectureFlow.caption: Synchronous calls block for a response; asynchronous events fan out to multiple consumers without the producer waiting.

simpleExample.scenario: An `Order` service calling a `Payment` service synchronously with OpenFeign.
simpleExample.input: A checkout request for a `Cart` with a total amount and a `Customer`'s payment method.
simpleExample.execution: `OrderService` calls `paymentClient.charge(...)`, which Feign turns into an HTTP `POST` to `payment-service`'s `/payments` endpoint, resolved via the service registry.
simpleExample.output: A `PaymentResponse` with status `SUCCESS`, returned synchronously before the order is confirmed.
simpleExample.explanation: Feign hides the HTTP plumbing behind a plain interface, but the caller still blocks until `payment-service` responds — a synchronous, tightly-coupled call.

realWorldExample.scenario: Decoupling `order-service` from `inventory-service` and `notification-service` using Kafka events instead of direct calls.
realWorldExample.input: A `Customer` completes checkout, creating an `Order` for several `Product` items.
realWorldExample.execution: `order-service` saves the order and publishes an `OrderPlacedEvent` to the `order-placed` Kafka topic, then returns immediately without waiting on any consumer.
realWorldExample.output: `inventory-service` and `notification-service` each independently consume the same event from their own consumer group and react on their own schedule.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is **Event-Driven Architecture**: `order-service` doesn't know or care who's listening, and a slow or temporarily-down consumer never blocks the checkout response.

comparisonTables.title: Saga: Choreography vs Orchestration
rows: Services react to each other's events
Harder to trace the full flow
Flow is explicit in one place

bestPractices: Prefer asynchronous events for anything that doesn't need an immediate response, to avoid cascading failures.
Use gRPC for high-throughput internal calls and REST for public-facing or browser-consumed APIs.
Put cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, SSL termination) at the **API Gateway**, not in every service.
commonMistakes: Making every service-to-service call synchronous by default, recreating monolith-style coupling over the network.
Letting clients call every microservice directly instead of going through a Gateway or BFF.
Choosing Choreography for a complex multi-step Saga, making the overall transaction flow impossible to trace.
interviewTips: Be ready to explain why synchronous calls cause **cascading failures** and how async communication avoids that.
Know the Saga pattern's two flavors by name — Choreography vs Orchestration — and when to use each.
Mention that WebClient replaced RestTemplate (deprecated in Spring 6) if asked about Spring's HTTP clients.

faq.q: When should I use gRPC instead of REST?
faq.a: Use gRPC for high-throughput, low-latency internal service-to-service calls where you control both ends; use REST when the API needs to be easily consumed by browsers, third parties, or ad-hoc tools.

faq.q: Does asynchronous communication mean the data is inconsistent?
faq.a: It means eventual consistency, not incorrect data — all consumers will eventually see the event and update their own state, just not at the exact same instant as the producer.

faq.q: Why use an API Gateway instead of exposing every service directly?
faq.a: A Gateway centralizes cross-cutting concerns like auth, rate limiting, and routing, so clients only need to know one entry point instead of every service's address and API contract.

summary: Services communicate synchronously (REST, gRPC, OpenFeign, WebClient) when an immediate response is needed, and asynchronously (Kafka, RabbitMQ) to decouple producers from consumers.
The Saga pattern coordinates distributed transactions across services via Choreography or Orchestration.
An API Gateway or Service Mesh centralizes routing, security, and observability so individual services stay focused on business logic.
quickRevision: Synchronous: REST, gRPC, OpenFeign, WebClient — caller blocks for a response.
Asynchronous: Kafka, RabbitMQ, event-driven — producer doesn't wait.
gRPC uses Protocol Buffers + HTTP/2; ideal for internal calls.
Saga Choreography = events react to events; Orchestration = central coordinator.
CQRS separates read and write models, often paired with Event Sourcing.
API Gateway = single entry point: routing, auth, rate limiting, SSL termination.
BFF = separate gateway per client type (web, mobile, IoT).
Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd) handles mTLS/retries without app code changes.

beginner: What's the difference between synchronous and asynchronous communication?
What is an API Gateway used for?
Name two synchronous and two asynchronous communication technologies.
intermediate: Why can synchronous calls cause cascading failures across services?
What is the Saga pattern, and why is it needed in microservices?
How does a Service Mesh differ from an API Gateway?
advanced: How would you choose between Saga Choreography and Orchestration for a multi-step checkout flow?
How does CQRS combined with Event Sourcing change how you model reads vs writes?
What trade-offs would push you to pick gRPC over REST for a new internal service?

title: Resilience Patterns
overview: Resilience patterns keep a system functioning, or failing gracefully, when a dependency is slow or unavailable.
The core patterns are Circuit Breaker, Retry, Timeout, Bulkhead, and Fallback.
Rate limiting protects services from being overwhelmed by too many requests, from clients or from other services.
why: In a distributed system, any of dozens of services can be slow, overloaded, or temporarily down at any moment.
Without protection, one failing dependency can exhaust threads and connections in every service that calls it.
Resilience patterns contain that failure so it doesn't spread across the whole system.
problem: A calling service that waits indefinitely on a slow dependency ties up its own threads.
As more requests pile up waiting, the calling service itself runs out of resources and becomes unresponsive.
This is a **cascading failure** — one unhealthy service drags down every service that depends on it.
Retrying blindly without any backoff can make an already-struggling service even worse (the **thundering herd**).

intro: Fault tolerance patterns and rate limiting work together to keep failures contained.

topics.heading: Fault Tolerance
points: **Circuit Breaker** — prevents cascading failures with three states: Closed (normal), Open (fail-fast), Half-Open (test recovery); use Resilience4j `@CircuitBreaker`.
**Retry** — automatically retries failed requests with backoff: `@Retry(name = "myService", fallbackMethod = "fallback")`, using exponential backoff to avoid a thundering herd.
**Timeout** — sets a max wait time for external calls via `@TimeLimiter(name = "myService")`, preventing threads from blocking indefinitely on slow services.
**Bulkhead** — isolates failures by limiting concurrent calls (thread pool or semaphore based), preventing one slow service from exhausting all resources.
**Fallback** — provides a degraded response when a service is unavailable, such as cached data, default values, or a graceful error message.

topics.heading: Rate Limiting
points: **Token Bucket** — tokens are added at a fixed rate and consumed per request, allowing burst traffic up to bucket capacity; the most common algorithm.
**Sliding Window** — tracks request count in a rolling time window; a fixed window has boundary issues, while a sliding window is more accurate.
**Gateway Rate Limiting** — apply rate limits at the API Gateway level using Spring Cloud Gateway's `RequestRateLimiter` filter with a Redis backend.
**HTTP 429 Too Many Requests** — the standard response for rate-limited requests, including a `Retry-After` header telling clients when to retry.
**Per-Client Limiting** — different rate limits per API key, user tier, or IP, using Redis sorted sets or in-memory caches for tracking.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed
    Closed --> Open: failure threshold exceeded
    Open --> HalfOpen: wait duration elapses
    HalfOpen --> Closed: test call succeeds
    HalfOpen --> Open: test call fails
    Closed --> Closed: call succeeds
architectureFlow.caption: Circuit Breaker state machine: Closed allows calls, Open fails fast, Half-Open cautiously tests if the dependency has recovered.

simpleExample.scenario: Protecting an `Order` service's call to a flaky `Payment` service with a Circuit Breaker and a Fallback.
simpleExample.input: A `PaymentRequest` for an `Order`, sent while `payment-service` is intermittently failing.
simpleExample.execution: Resilience4j counts recent failures; once the failure rate crosses the configured threshold, the circuit trips to **Open** and every subsequent call short-circuits straight to `fallbackCharge` without hitting the network.
simpleExample.output: A `PaymentResponse` marked `PENDING` returned instantly, instead of the caller hanging until a real timeout.
simpleExample.explanation: The Circuit Breaker stops `order-service` from repeatedly hammering an already-struggling `payment-service`, and the Fallback keeps the checkout flow responsive.

realWorldExample.scenario: Combining Retry, Timeout, and Bulkhead around a call from `order-service` to a third-party `Gateway` for a `Merchant`'s payment.
realWorldExample.input: A `PaymentRequest` routed to an external payment `Gateway` that is occasionally slow under load.
realWorldExample.execution: **Timeout** caps how long each attempt can take; **Retry** retries a failed attempt with exponential backoff; **Bulkhead** caps how many concurrent gateway calls can run at once so one slow burst can't exhaust every thread; **Circuit Breaker** stops calling entirely once failures spike.
realWorldExample.output: Either a successful `PaymentResponse`, or a clean `failed` response from the fallback — never a thread hung indefinitely on the gateway.
realWorldExample.explanation: Layering all four patterns together is standard production practice: each one guards against a different failure mode (slow response, transient blip, resource exhaustion, sustained outage).

comparisonTables.title: Retry vs Circuit Breaker vs Bulkhead
rows: Fail fast once a threshold is crossed
Cap concurrent calls via thread pool/semaphore

comparisonTables.title: Token Bucket vs Sliding Window
rows: Allows bursts up to bucket capacity
More accurate than a fixed window

bestPractices: Layer Timeout, Retry, Circuit Breaker, and Bulkhead together — each guards against a different failure mode.
Always use exponential backoff on Retry to avoid a thundering herd against a recovering service.
Return a `Retry-After` header alongside HTTP 429 so well-behaved clients know exactly when to retry.
commonMistakes: Retrying without backoff, which can overwhelm a service that's already struggling to recover.
Setting no Timeout at all, letting a slow dependency block threads indefinitely.
Applying rate limits only at the service level and not at the Gateway, letting bad traffic reach every downstream service.
interviewTips: Know the three Circuit Breaker states by name — Closed, Open, Half-Open — and what triggers each transition.
Be ready to explain the thundering herd problem and why exponential backoff fixes it.
If asked to design resilience for a call chain, mention layering multiple patterns, not just one.

faq.q: What's the difference between a Timeout and a Circuit Breaker?
faq.a: A Timeout limits how long a single call can take; a Circuit Breaker tracks failures across many calls over time and stops calling entirely once a threshold is crossed.

faq.q: Why does a Circuit Breaker have a Half-Open state instead of just going straight back to Closed?
faq.a: Half-Open lets a small number of test calls through to check if the dependency has actually recovered, without immediately sending it the full load again.

faq.q: Is Bulkhead the same thing as a Circuit Breaker?
faq.a: No — Bulkhead limits concurrent calls to prevent resource exhaustion, while Circuit Breaker stops calls entirely based on failure rate; they're often used together, not as alternatives.

summary: Circuit Breaker, Retry, Timeout, Bulkhead, and Fallback each guard against a different way a dependency can fail.
The Circuit Breaker's Closed/Open/Half-Open state machine is the central mechanism for stopping cascading failures.
Rate limiting (Token Bucket, Sliding Window) protects services from being overwhelmed, usually enforced at the API Gateway.
quickRevision: Circuit Breaker states: Closed (normal) → Open (fail-fast) → Half-Open (test recovery).
Retry needs exponential backoff to avoid a thundering herd.
Timeout prevents threads blocking indefinitely on slow calls.
Bulkhead isolates concurrent calls via thread pool or semaphore.
Fallback returns a degraded response instead of an error.
Token Bucket allows bursts; Sliding Window is more accurate over time.
HTTP 429 + `Retry-After` header is the standard rate-limit response.
Resilience4j annotations: `@CircuitBreaker`, `@Retry`, `@TimeLimiter`, `@Bulkhead`.

beginner: What problem does a Circuit Breaker solve?
What are the three states of a Circuit Breaker?
What is rate limiting, and why is it needed?
intermediate: Why is exponential backoff important when implementing Retry?
How does Bulkhead prevent one slow dependency from affecting the whole service?
What's the difference between Token Bucket and Sliding Window rate limiting?
advanced: How would you combine Retry, Timeout, Circuit Breaker, and Bulkhead for a single external call?
How do you decide the failure threshold and wait duration for a Circuit Breaker in production?
How would you design per-client rate limiting at the API Gateway level using Redis?

title: Spring Cloud Stack
overview: Spring Cloud provides the building blocks for running microservices: service discovery, centralized configuration, and distributed tracing.
Services need to find each other by name instead of hardcoded addresses.
Configuration and tracing need to work consistently across many independently deployed services.
why: Service instances come and go constantly — scaled up, scaled down, restarted, redeployed with new IPs.
Hardcoding addresses would break the moment any instance moved, which defeats independent deployability.
Debugging a request that touches five services needs a way to follow it across all of them.
problem: Without service discovery, every service needs to know every other service's exact network address.
Without centralized configuration, changing one setting means redeploying every instance that uses it.
Without distributed tracing, a slow or failing request is nearly impossible to diagnose across service boundaries.

intro: Spring Cloud addresses this with three pillars: discovery, configuration, and tracing.

topics.heading: Service Discovery
points: **Eureka Server** — Netflix OSS service registry; services register on startup and send heartbeats, clients discover services by name.
**Spring Cloud LoadBalancer** — client-side load balancing that replaces Netflix Ribbon, using round-robin or random instance selection from the registry.
**Consul** — HashiCorp service discovery plus config store plus health checking; an alternative to Eureka with a built-in KV store and multi-datacenter support.
**Service Registration** — `@EnableDiscoveryClient` on a service auto-registers it with the configured registry, using `spring.application.name` for service identity.
**Health Checks** — the registry removes unhealthy instances: Eureka uses heartbeats, Consul uses HTTP/TCP/script checks, both fed by the Actuator `/health` endpoint.

topics.heading: Configuration Management
points: **Spring Cloud Config Server** — centralized external configuration backed by Git, file system, or Vault, serving config via REST to client services.
**`@RefreshScope`** — refreshes bean properties without a restart, triggered via `/actuator/refresh` or Spring Cloud Bus for a broadcast refresh across instances.
**HashiCorp Vault** — secure secrets management with dynamic secrets, encryption as a service, and lease-based credentials, via Spring Vault integration.
**Config Profiles** — `application-{profile}.yml` per environment, activated with `spring.profiles.active`; the Config Server serves profile-specific overrides.
**Spring Cloud Bus** — broadcasts config changes to all instances via a message broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka) so a single refresh call propagates everywhere.

topics.heading: Distributed Tracing
points: **Micrometer Tracing** — a vendor-neutral observability facade (replacing Spring Cloud Sleuth) that generates trace and span IDs for request correlation.
**Zipkin** — a distributed tracing system that collects timing data, visualizes call chains, and identifies latency bottlenecks across services.
**OpenTelemetry** — the CNCF standard for traces, metrics, and logs, with an auto-instrumentation agent that exports to Jaeger, Zipkin, or any OTLP backend.
**Log Correlation** — include `traceId` and `spanId` in log patterns via MDC (`%X{traceId}` in Logback), enabling searching logs across services by trace.
**Span Propagation** — trace context is propagated via HTTP headers (W3C Trace Context or B3), automatic with Spring instrumentation libraries.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["payment-service starts up"] --> B["Registers with Eureka"]
    B --> C["Sends periodic heartbeat"]
    D["order-service needs Payment"] --> E["Queries Eureka by service name"]
    E --> F["Load Balancer picks an instance"]
    F --> G["order-service calls payment-service"]
    H["Config changes in Git"] --> I["Spring Cloud Bus broadcasts refresh"]
    I --> A
architectureFlow.caption: A service registers and sends heartbeats to Eureka; callers discover it by name, and config changes broadcast to every instance via Spring Cloud Bus.

simpleExample.scenario: Registering `payment-service` with Eureka so `order-service` can discover it by name instead of a hardcoded URL.
simpleExample.input: The service starts up with `spring.application.name=payment-service` configured.
simpleExample.execution: `@EnableDiscoveryClient` registers the instance with the Eureka server on startup and sends a heartbeat on a fixed interval to prove it's alive.
simpleExample.output: `payment-service` appears in the Eureka dashboard as a healthy, discoverable instance under that exact name.
simpleExample.explanation: Any other service can now call `payment-service` by name via a load-balanced client instead of a fixed IP that would break on redeploy.

realWorldExample.scenario: `order-service` calling `payment-service` through service discovery, with centralized config and distributed tracing wired in.
realWorldExample.input: A `PaymentRequest` from `order-service`, plus a config change to `payment.max-amount` pushed to the Config Server's Git backend.
realWorldExample.execution: `PaymentClient` resolves `payment-service`'s address via Eureka and calls it; the request is automatically tagged with a `traceId`/`spanId` propagated via HTTP headers and visible in Zipkin. Separately, Spring Cloud Bus broadcasts the `payment.max-amount` change to every `@RefreshScope` bean across all instances.
realWorldExample.output: The call to `payment-service` succeeds with full trace visibility end-to-end, and the new `maxAmount` takes effect on every instance without a redeploy.
realWorldExample.explanation: This combines all three pillars: discovery finds the instance, Config Server plus Bus propagates config changes live, and tracing makes the cross-service call visible for debugging.

comparisonTables.title: Eureka vs Consul
rows: Can serve as config store itself
Polyglot environments needing more than discovery

bestPractices: Always set `spring.application.name` explicitly — it's the identity every other service discovers you by.
Use Spring Cloud Bus with Config Server so a single config change propagates to every instance automatically.
Propagate `traceId`/`spanId` into logs via MDC so cross-service issues can be traced from log aggregation tools alone.
commonMistakes: Hardcoding a service's address instead of relying on discovery, breaking the moment that instance is redeployed.
Storing secrets directly in Config Server-backed Git files instead of delegating to Vault.
Forgetting `@RefreshScope`, so config changes never actually apply until the service restarts.
interviewTips: Know that Spring Cloud LoadBalancer replaced Netflix Ribbon, and Micrometer Tracing replaced Spring Cloud Sleuth — naming the old tool shows depth.
Be ready to describe the full loop: register → heartbeat → discover → load-balance → call.
Mention Spring Cloud Bus by name if asked how a config change reaches every running instance at once.

faq.q: Do I need Eureka if I'm running on Kubernetes?
faq.a: Not necessarily — Kubernetes' own Service and DNS-based discovery can replace Eureka, but Eureka is still common in Spring-centric or non-Kubernetes deployments.

faq.q: What's the difference between Config Server profiles and Kubernetes ConfigMaps?
faq.a: They solve the same problem at different layers — Config Server externalizes Spring configuration via Git, while ConfigMaps externalize configuration at the container/orchestration layer; some teams use only one, others layer both.

faq.q: Why do I need distributed tracing if I already have logs from every service?
faq.a: Individual service logs don't show you the full path of a single request across five services — a trace ID ties all of those separate log lines together into one coherent timeline.

summary: Service Discovery (Eureka, Consul) lets services find each other by name instead of hardcoded addresses.
Spring Cloud Config Server plus Spring Cloud Bus centralizes configuration and propagates changes live across instances.
Distributed tracing (Micrometer Tracing, Zipkin, OpenTelemetry) makes a single request traceable across every service it touches.
quickRevision: Eureka: services register + heartbeat; clients discover by name.
Spring Cloud LoadBalancer replaced Netflix Ribbon for client-side load balancing.
Consul = discovery + config + health checks + multi-datacenter, alternative to Eureka.
`@EnableDiscoveryClient` + `spring.application.name` = service identity.
Config Server centralizes config, backed by Git/file system/Vault.
`@RefreshScope` + Spring Cloud Bus = live config refresh across all instances, no restart.
Micrometer Tracing replaced Spring Cloud Sleuth for trace/span generation.
Trace context propagates via HTTP headers (W3C Trace Context or B3).

beginner: What problem does service discovery solve?
What is Spring Cloud Config Server used for?
What is distributed tracing, and why is it needed in microservices?
intermediate: How does a service register itself with Eureka, and how does the registry know it's still alive?
How does `@RefreshScope` combined with Spring Cloud Bus refresh config across many instances at once?
What's the difference between Eureka and Consul?
advanced: How would you propagate a trace ID across a synchronous call and an asynchronous Kafka event in the same request flow?
What are the trade-offs of using Kubernetes-native discovery versus Eureka?
How would you secure secrets served through Config Server using Vault instead of storing them in Git?

title: Deployment & Monitoring
overview: Deploying microservices means packaging each service into a container and running it on an orchestrator like Kubernetes.
Observability — logs, metrics, health checks, and alerts — is what makes many small services operable in production.
This section covers containerization, Kubernetes, and the tools used to see what's actually happening across a fleet of services.
why: Dozens of independently deployed services need a consistent, repeatable way to be packaged and run.
Manually managing scaling, restarts, and rollouts across many services doesn't scale with team size.
With many moving parts, you can't reason about system health by reading a handful of application logs anymore.
problem: Running a JVM app used to mean installing a specific JDK, dependencies, and an app server directly on a machine.
"Works on my machine" failures were common when environments drifted between dev, staging, and prod.
Manually scaling instances up and down in response to load doesn't scale to dozens of independently deployed services.
Without centralized logs and metrics, diagnosing an issue means SSHing into individual machines one at a time.

intro: Deployment for microservices spans three layers: containers, orchestration, and observability.

topics.heading: Containerization
points: **Dockerfile** — `FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine` for a minimal image; copy the JAR, expose the port, set the entrypoint, and use `.dockerignore` to exclude files.
**Multi-Stage Build** — a build stage compiles with Maven/Gradle, then a runtime stage copies only the JAR, significantly reducing the final image size.
**Docker Compose** — defines multi-container setups with service dependencies, shared networks, and volume mounts; ideal for local development with databases and queues.
**Jib** — builds optimized Docker images without a Dockerfile, as a Maven/Gradle plugin by Google, with layer caching for fast rebuilds.
**Health & Graceful Shutdown** — `HEALTHCHECK` in the Dockerfile, plus `server.shutdown=graceful` and `spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase` in Spring Boot.

topics.heading: Kubernetes
points: **Deployment** — manages ReplicaSets and rolling updates; you define the desired state (replicas, image, resources) and K8s ensures the actual state matches.
**Service** — a stable network endpoint for Pods: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (external), LoadBalancer (cloud), with DNS-based service discovery.
**ConfigMap & Secret** — externalize configuration, mounted as environment variables or volumes; Secrets are base64-encoded (use Sealed Secrets for actual encryption).
**HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)** — auto-scales pods based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics via `minReplicas`, `maxReplicas`, `targetCPUUtilizationPercentage`.
**Helm** — the Kubernetes package manager; charts define templated manifests with values files per environment (`helm install`, `helm upgrade --set`).

topics.heading: Observability
points: **Logging (ELK Stack)** — Elasticsearch stores logs, Logstash/Fluentd collects and transforms them, Kibana visualizes them; structured JSON logging is recommended.
**Metrics (Prometheus + Grafana)** — Prometheus scrapes `/actuator/prometheus` metrics, Grafana dashboards visualize them, and alert rules catch anomalies.
**Health Checks** — Actuator `/health` for liveness and `/health/readiness` for readiness probes, plus custom health indicators for downstream dependencies.
**Alerting** — Prometheus Alertmanager or Grafana alerts define thresholds for error rate, p99 latency, and pod restarts, routed to Slack, PagerDuty, or email.
**SLOs and SLIs** — define Service Level Objectives (e.g. 99.9% availability, p99 < 200ms), tracked with Service Level Indicators; error budgets guide risk decisions.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["CI builds Order Service JAR"] --> B["Docker multi-stage build"]
    B --> C["Push image to registry"]
    C --> D["Kubernetes rolling deployment"]
    D --> E["Pods pass liveness/readiness probes"]
    E --> F["Prometheus scrapes metrics"]
    F --> G["Grafana dashboards + Alertmanager"]
architectureFlow.caption: From CI build to a running, monitored Kubernetes deployment: build, containerize, push, roll out, then observe.

simpleExample.scenario: A minimal multi-stage Dockerfile for an `order-service` Spring Boot JAR.
simpleExample.code:
# Build stage
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN ./mvnw package -DskipTests

# Runtime stage
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app/target/order-service.jar app.jar
EXPOSE 8080
HEALTHCHECK CMD wget -q --spider http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]
simpleExample.input: The `order-service` source code and a `pom.xml` built by CI.
simpleExample.execution: The `build` stage compiles the JAR with Maven inside a JDK image; the final runtime stage starts from a lean JRE image and copies in only the compiled JAR, discarding the build tools.
simpleExample.output: A small runtime image containing just a JRE and the application JAR, with a `HEALTHCHECK` polling `/actuator/health`.
simpleExample.explanation: Multi-stage builds keep the shipped image lean by never including Maven, source code, or build caches in the final layer.

realWorldExample.scenario: Deploying `order-service` to Kubernetes with autoscaling, health probes, and Prometheus metrics wired in.
realWorldExample.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: order-service
          image: registry/order-service:1.4.0
          ports: [{ containerPort: 8080 }]
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/liveness, port: 8080 }
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/readiness, port: 8080 }
---
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: order-service-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef: { kind: Deployment, name: order-service }
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource: { name: cpu, target: { type: Utilization, averageUtilization: 70 } }
realWorldExample.input: A new `order-service:1.4.0` image pushed to the registry after a merchant checkout feature ships.
realWorldExample.execution: Kubernetes performs a rolling update to the `Deployment`, routing traffic only to pods that pass the `readiness` probe; the `HorizontalPodAutoscaler` watches CPU utilization and scales between 3 and 10 replicas as `Order` traffic rises and falls; Prometheus scrapes each pod's `/actuator/prometheus` endpoint the whole time.
realWorldExample.output: A zero-downtime rollout to the new version, automatic scale-out during a flash sale, and Grafana dashboards showing p99 latency and error rate throughout.
realWorldExample.explanation: Liveness restarts a stuck pod, readiness stops traffic from hitting a pod before it's ready, and the HPA plus Prometheus/Grafana together keep the service both elastic and observable in production.

comparisonTables.title: Liveness vs Readiness Probes
rows: Is the app ready to receive traffic?
Kubernetes stops routing traffic to it
Detect startup warm-up or downstream dependency issues

bestPractices: Always use multi-stage Docker builds to keep runtime images small and free of build tooling.
Configure both liveness and readiness probes — they answer different questions and both matter.
Define SLOs and error budgets up front so alert thresholds are based on agreed targets, not guesses.
commonMistakes: Using only a liveness probe and skipping readiness, causing traffic to hit pods that aren't actually ready yet.
Storing real secrets in a Kubernetes `Secret` without additional encryption, forgetting that base64 is not encryption.
Setting HPA `minReplicas`/`maxReplicas` without load testing, leading to either over-provisioning or scaling too late.
interviewTips: Be precise about the liveness vs readiness distinction — it's a very common follow-up question.
Know that Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded, not encrypted, and mention Sealed Secrets as the fix.
If asked about observability, structure your answer around logs, metrics, and traces as three distinct pillars.

faq.q: Why use Jib instead of a Dockerfile?
faq.a: Jib builds optimized, reproducible images directly from a Maven/Gradle build with layer caching, without needing a Docker daemon or hand-written Dockerfile — useful for faster, more consistent CI builds.

faq.q: What happens if a pod fails its liveness probe?
faq.a: Kubernetes considers the container unhealthy and restarts it, on the assumption that the process is stuck or deadlocked and a restart is the fastest recovery.

faq.q: Why do I need both Prometheus/Grafana and an ELK stack?
faq.a: They cover different observability needs — metrics (Prometheus/Grafana) show trends and trigger threshold-based alerts, while logs (ELK) let you inspect the specific events and context around an incident.

summary: Containerization (Docker, multi-stage builds, Jib) packages each service into a small, consistent, portable image.
Kubernetes manages deployment, scaling (HPA), configuration (ConfigMap/Secret), and networking (Service) for those containers.
Observability — ELK for logs, Prometheus/Grafana for metrics, health checks, and SLOs — makes a fleet of services operable in production.
quickRevision: Multi-stage Docker builds keep runtime images small by discarding build tools.
Jib builds images without a Dockerfile, with fast layer caching.
K8s Deployment manages ReplicaSets and rolling updates.
K8s Service types: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort, LoadBalancer (external).
HPA auto-scales pods on CPU/memory/custom metrics.
Helm = Kubernetes package manager with templated charts.
Liveness probe restarts a stuck pod; readiness probe controls traffic routing.
ELK = logs; Prometheus + Grafana = metrics; both feed alerting.
SLOs/SLIs and error budgets guide how much risk you can take before it matters.

beginner: What is a multi-stage Docker build, and why use one?
What's the difference between a liveness and a readiness probe?
intermediate: How does a Kubernetes Deployment perform a rolling update?
What's the difference between a ConfigMap and a Secret, and why aren't Secrets fully secure by default?
How do Prometheus and Grafana work together for monitoring?
advanced: How would you design health checks so a slow downstream dependency doesn't kill an otherwise-healthy pod?
How would you set HPA thresholds and probe timings for a service with a slow JVM warm-up?
How would you define and enforce an SLO with an error budget for a production microservice?
